The earthquake shook the ovens so hard that all my sourdough starters spilled across the floor.
I need to find a way to keep the yeast warm while the power is out.
There is so much flour dust in the air after the ceiling collapsed that I can barely breathe.
I will bake as many loaves as possible today to feed the neighbors who lost their homes.
The floodwaters are rising fast and I cannot leave my expensive deck ovens behind to be ruined.
The oven is running hot, but that violet light in the sky makes my sourdough look grey.
I can't tell if the yeast is reacting to the humidity or that humming sound from the stars.
My timers are all screaming at once, but I can't stop staring at the moon splitting open.
If the power goes out because of those flashes, I will lose three batches of brioche.
The flour on my apron is glowing a pale green under that strange, shimmering celestial curtain.
You cannot move that fence because it blocks the delivery path for my sacks of flour.
My outdoor cooling racks are sitting right on the line, and I need that space for my loaves.
If you encroach on this strip of land, I will have no room for my wood-fired oven.
I cannot have your garden soil blowing into my kneading area every time the wind picks up.
We need to settle this boundary now so I can finish building the new pantry extension.
I hope this shimmering rift doesn't mess with the humidity levels for my sourdough.
If I step through that glowing hole, will I still have a hot oven waiting?
The air coming from that portal smells faintly of ozone and burnt sugar.
I can't leave my proofing baskets behind just because a magical doorway appeared.
I wonder if the flour in another dimension is finely milled enough for cake.
I hope I can find some dry wood to build a proper oven before nightfall.
This thick humidity is going to make my dough rise far too quickly if I stop.
I must keep my flour sacks elevated and dry, or the damp earth will ruin them.
My hands are used to kneading dough, not hacking through these stubborn, thorny vines.
If I can find some wild berries, I might be able to bake a tart.
I can hear the sourdough starter bubbling in the jar while the room stays deathly quiet.
The only sound in this kitchen is the rhythmic thumping of my palms kneading the dough.
I keep glancing at the oven timer, waiting for a bell to break this heavy, oppressive silence.
It feels strange to work through the morning rush without the usual chatter of the delivery drivers.
I find myself listening to the faint crackle of cooling crusts because nobody has spoken for hours.
Get away from my ovens before you ruin the temperature for the sourdough.
I will throw a tray of scorching hot biscuits at you if you step closer.
You are not taking my secret recipe or burning down this bakery today.
Back off or I'll use this heavy rolling pin to knock some sense into you.
Leave now or you will find out how hard a cast iron skillet hits.
This seasoned cast-iron dutch oven has given me the most consistent sourdough crusts for a decade.
My grandfather's heavy wooden proofing basket is the only tool that shapes my boules perfectly every time.
I cherish this vintage copper mixing bowl because it conducts heat exactly how I need for my meringues.
This digital scale is my most trusted companion when I need precision down to the single gram.
My industrial deck oven is the heart of this shop and the secret to my signature crumb.
I only have two bags of flour left and the morning rush hasn't even started yet.
I need to decide if I should save this last bit of yeast for the sourdough or the brioche.
The oven space is too tight to fit all these loaves before the breakfast crowd arrives.
I cannot waste a single gram of butter if I want to finish the pastry order today.
There is not enough fuel in the wood stove to keep the heat consistent for another batch.
